suggestion: Replace the parseUse function body with `const parseUse = makeStoreParser("use");` alongside parseAny/parseNotAny/parseOuts.
rationale: makeStoreParser builds `^${keyword}\s*\(\s*(${KMN_IDENT})\s*\)$` (case-insensitive) and returns `m ? (m[1] ?? null) : null`. parseUse constructs the byte-identical regex and returns the byte-identical expression, so it is a hand-inlined copy of makeStoreParser("use"). Reusing the factory also removes the per-call `new RegExp` allocation. No /g flag; behavior identical.

summary: KNOWN_MODIFIERS Set rebuilt on every parseVkeyBracket call
suggestion: Hoist the KNOWN_MODIFIERS Set to a module-scope const; parseVkeyBracket only reads it.
rationale: parseVkeyBracket runs for every [..] bracket token across context/store/output parsing, and each call allocates a fresh 10-element Set. The set is static and read-only, so a single module-scope Set is equivalent. No state, no /g concern.

summary: parseIndex builds a new RegExp on every call
suggestion: Compile the index regex once into a module-scope const (KMN_IDENT is already a module const) and reference it inside parseIndex.
rationale: The pattern interpolates only the constant KMN_IDENT, so it is invariant across calls; recompiling it per token is wasted work. The regex has no /g flag and .exec is used non-cumulatively, so hoisting is safe (matches the makeStoreParser pattern already used for any/notany/outs).

suggestion: Hoist each of the three `new RegExp(...)` (store, group, begin line matchers) to module-scope consts; all interpolate only the constant KMN_IDENT.
rationale: Each patterns is invariant (only KMN_IDENT interpolated) and none use /g, so a single compiled instance per pattern is behavior-preserving. Lower call frequency than parse-02/03 (once per store/group/begin line), hence needs-human rather than a slam-dunk, but genuine redundant recompilation.

summary: p.toUpperCase() computed twice per modifier part
suggestion: Compute `const up = p.toUpperCase();` once, then test `KNOWN_MODIFIERS.has(up)` and push `up`.
rationale: Inside parseVkeyBracket the loop calls p.toUpperCase() for the membership test and again for the push. Caching the single result is a pure micro-cleanup with identical output.

summary: Stale comment references a non-existent closure
suggestion: Drop/trim the 'Store the encoding string ... Use a local variable captured by the closure below.' comment — beginEncoding is assigned directly, there is no closure.
rationale: The code assigns `beginEncoding = parsed.encoding` inline; no closure captures it. The comment describes an earlier design and is now misleading. Comment-only, behavior-preserving.

summary: if/save-set-reset/call-return opaque-directive classification duplicated across three parsers
suggestion: Extract a helper `directiveOpaqueReason(tok): OpaqueReason | null` returning IF_OPTION_STORE / OPTION_STORE_DIRECTIVE / CALL_RETURN, and call it from parseStoreItems, parseContextElements, and the strict branch of parseOutputElementsCore.
rationale: The same three regex tests (`/^if\s*\(/i`, `/^(?:save|set|reset)\s*\(/i`, `/^call\s*\(/i` || `/^return$/i`) mapping to the same three OPAQUE_REASONS appear verbatim in all three functions. Consolidating removes the copy-paste. Marked needs-human because the output-core branch also emits a {kind:'raw'} element in lenient mode, so the extraction must preserve the strict-vs-lenient divergence per call site.

summary: splitTokens(parsedLine.outputRaw) is recomputed at line 904 though outToks was already computed at line 885.
suggestion: Hoist outToks above the ctxElements check (or reuse the existing binding) so the opaque produced-output sketch at line 904 reuses it instead of re-splitting the same string.
rationale: When ctxElements!==null but outElements===null, outToks (line 885) and the re-split at line 904 are the identical pure computation over the same string, so reusing the first result is byte-identical. Needs-human because outToks is currently scoped inside the ctxElements!==null block and a small restructure is required to reuse it safely.
